[00:00:03 - 00:00:33] Speaker 2
the April 8th meeting of the Metropolitan King County Council's Budget and Fiscal Management Committee. I am Rod Demboski, chairing the committee this year, along with our Vice Chair, Council Member Barone. Thank you for all being here. We are doing this as an all remote meeting today because we only have two items in their briefing items. And I wanted to respect those folks that may have spring break family obligations. So try to make it as easy as possible and do it remote. And we welcome you. We will start with a roll call.

[00:00:34 - 00:00:35] Speaker 10
Thank you, Chair.

[00:00:35 - 00:00:58] Speaker 2
Council Member Buton? Here. Council Member Fain? Here. Council Member Lewis? Here. Council Member Mosqueda? Here. Council Member Perry? Here. Chair Demboski? Here. All right, sounds like we have one hundred attendance. Thanks for being here. We will turn to item three, approval of the meeting minutes from March 25th. Council

[00:00:58 - 00:01:04] Speaker 12
Member Brown. Chair, I move that we approve the minutes of the March 25th, 2026 minutes.

[00:01:04 - 00:01:52] Speaker 2
Okay, all in favor, please say aye. Aye. Any opposed? Those are approved. We will turn to public comment and I will start first with anybody in chambers and then go to online. uh for those that may be new while we are having our clerk call the folks up we do have comments usually limited to two minutes so we ask that you keep uh keep your remarks within two minutes and i think folks generally know the rules so i am not going to repeat those i am sorry are you waiting for Angelica, will you help us with our list of folks who signed up for public comment? We will start first with. I am

[00:01:52 - 00:02:08] Speaker 8
so sorry. I was promoting council members. I missed that part. Yes. My apologies. We have three people in chambers and I have no one yet raised their hand. And now I have two people that one people that has raised their hand on Zoom.

[00:02:08 - 00:02:15] Speaker 2
All right, great. We will start with those in chambers. And then if you are online and want to give comments, just raise your hand and Angelica will call on you at that right time.

[00:02:17 - 00:02:20] Speaker 8
Thank you. So we will start with Alex Zimmerman. Thank you.

[00:02:25 - 00:02:35] Speaker 1
Hi, my name Alex Zimmerman and I live here for forty years.

[00:02:35 - 00:02:45] Speaker 2
Yeah, Alex, you know the rules about the sign. You were not having signs at podiums. So you can put that down and continue your remarks, or you can end your remarks. Thank you so much. It

Yeah, Alex, you know the rules about the sign. You were not having signs at podiums. So you can put that down and continue your remarks, or you can end your remarks. Thank you so much. It

[00:02:45 - 00:03:04] Speaker 1
is okay. No problem, Mayor. Thank you. My name, Alex Zimmerman. I live here for forty years. I am president of Stand Up America. I am a fifteen-time candidate for election. the government prosecute me seven times all cases dismissed and i have seven thousand day of trespass nobody in america

so alex uh you know none of that is on the agenda so uh you are

[00:03:09 - 00:04:29] Speaker 1
absolutely right you are absolutely right i want to address who i am and right now we will speak about agenda yes absolutely yeah so about agenda right now this agenda is always money yeah i do not know how much money you have Because every time I come here for the last thirty five years or thirty years, you know what I mean? You are talking about budget, money, budget, money, and always money not enough. You always make more and more taxes. So I try to understand how much money you government need in country. You know what this means? Country government who you represent right now, all council. So you will be happy. You are always not happy. And I do not understand why. Billion and billion dollars spent every year for nothing. It is exactly my opinion. When you come to detail, you know what it means. No something positive I see here in King County for last thirty years. Negative, negative, negative. No money, not enough money, not enough money, nothing changed. Housing. Senior citizen, disabled, homeless, nothing. Viva Trump, viva new American revolution. Stand up, slave, and happy cow. How much do you need? Thank you very much.

[00:04:32 - 00:04:35] Speaker 8
Thank you. Next, we have Tyler Vasquez.

[00:04:49 - 00:06:50] Speaker 13
good morning council members my name is tyler vasquez i am here on behalf of cascade bicycle club in support of the grand connection crossing this project is the kind of regional infrastructure investment king county should be making it connects people not just places If we want more people to walk and bike, we need to build safe, comfortable connections for people to use. The Grand Connection Crossing will provide a safe, car-free route over I-four hundred five, linking downtown Bellevue, the county's second largest job center, with forty two miles of the East Rail Corridor. For families living in affordable housing in Wilburton and communities stretching from Woodinville to Redton and South King County. This crossing removes a real barrier to economic opportunity. It means reaching jobs, childcare, healthcare, and essential services by walking, biking, and rolling without needing a car. Bellevue has committed to fifty seven,zero units of income-restricted housing in this corridor by 2036. The Grand Connection Crossing does not just complement that investment, it makes it work. Affordable housing near a freeway disconnected from jobs and transit is not a genuine opportunity. This crossing makes it easier on their communities and commutes. beyond access to work the crossing opens connections to one hundred seventy five miles of regional corridor the mountain to sound greenway cedar river trail green river lake to sound and the five hundred twenty corridor these are public amenities that are historically been out of reach for lower income communities this project brings them within reach The crossing is in Bellevue, but its benefits are regional. It advances the community's equity goals by ensuring all residents, regardless of income or car, can access their healthy lives. We ask the county to support this project. Thank you.

[00:06:53 - 00:06:56] Speaker 8
Thank you. Next, we have Kurt Hobinkover.

[00:06:58 - 00:06:59] Speaker 1
About what I just keep talking about, right?

[00:07:02 - 00:08:59] Speaker 7
Hi. Good morning, council members. I am Kirk Hovincotter, the executive director of Transportation Choices Coalition. We are Washington State's nonprofit advocate for transit and safe walks to transit. T C C strongly supports the Grand Connection Crossing, and we are pleased to join regional and statewide advocates in urging King County to support and invest in this project. we worked hard to make light rail to and across the east side a reality and as chair dembowski's background reflects the cross lake connection recently opened bringing light rail to bellevue redmond and across the county expanding access to a regional network for thousands of transit riders but that investment only reaches its full potential when people can get to it Everyone's transit trip starts when they leave their front door. The Grand Connection crossing helps make that possible to get across the gap that is four hundred five in Bellevue. On Monday, in this beautiful seventy-degree sunny weather, I took the two-line to Bellevue and rode the East Trail on my bike. After I got off the train, I met a father who rides his e-bike from Kirkland to the Wilburton station every day to work, but then hops on the train to go the last stop, just one stop to get to downtown Bellevue to get to his job. He did that because he did not feel comfortable riding across 405. The Grand Connection would change that. It would provide that father a safe way to get on his bike the entire way to downtown Bellevue. A safe, car-free connection over four hundred five linking the East Trail to downtown Bellevue and its light rail stations means more people, especially those who rely on transit, can reach jobs, healthcare, and services across the county. It is a meaningful extension of the light rail investments this region has already made. We urge you to support this meaningful investment in safe, multimodal connections. Thank you for your time.

[00:09:01 - 00:09:07] Speaker 8
Thank you. Now, Chair, we are going to go to people online. I have Brady Nostrom.

[00:09:11 - 00:09:16] Speaker 14
Okay, go ahead, Brady. Greetings. My name is Brady Nostrom.

[00:09:20 - 00:09:24] Speaker 2
They do not flip the camera on. Okay. There is been some problems.

[00:09:25 - 00:11:12] Speaker 14
Makes sense. Well, greetings, Chair Dombrowski and members of the Budget and Fiscal Management Committee. My name is Brady Nordstrom, and I am the Associate Director of Government Relations and Policy at the Housing Development Consortium. I am here in support of the Bellevue Grand Connection project and King County's participation in the tax increment financing proposal as a critical funding tool to make this project a reality. So H G C's mission is to increase the production and preservation of affordable housing through throughout the county. And we envision a region where everyone has access to safe, stable and affordable home, especially close to jobs, schools and other community. assets and so over the last several years hcc's worked through a broad-based coalition of unlikely partners in the east side known as the east side housing round table and this work led to a consensus agreement around a historic land use code update in the wilberton neighborhood This added significant new housing and jobs capacity close to new light rail, close to the downtown employment centers, schools, and other public amenities like the Bellevue Botanical Garden. And it also, and for us, this was super important, it included a mandatory affordable housing requirement in Wilberton that ensures that the neighborhood will include affordable housing as a portion of growth. And this is the first policy of its kind in Bellevue since 1996. So H T C supports the Grand Connection as a transformative project that can catalyze housing development in the Wilberton neighborhood, which brings housing generally closer to jobs and amenities, but also affordable housing in Bellevue. And ultimately, we want this to make Bellevue more accessible to workers and families that cannot currently find an affordable home within the city limits. So thank you very much for the chance to speak today. Thank you.

[00:11:14 - 00:11:18] Speaker 8
Thank you. Next is Nate Caminos.

[00:11:21 - 00:11:22] Speaker 11
All right, Nate.

[00:11:22 - 00:11:22] Speaker 2
Good morning.

[00:11:24 - 00:13:58] Speaker 11
Good morning, Chair and Dembowski and fellow committee members. My name is Nate Caminos. Many of you know me for my day job as a government relations director at the Port of Seattle. But I am here today as the board president of East Trail Partners, the 501c3 nonprofit that works closely with all the owners of the forty two miles of the East Trail. including King County. I am here this morning to express our organization's strong support for the Grand Connection project, be the second item on your briefing agenda today. I also want to note that our executive director, Catherine Hollis, sits on the executive committee of the Friends of the Grand Connection crossing, reflecting the deep partnerships between our organizations. You know, back in 2014, the Seattle Chamber hosted an intercity study mission in New York City, and there we had an opportunity to tour the Highline project. It was interesting to see the corporate partnership support reflected in the development maintenance and operations of that project. And I remember soon after the Regional Advisory Council convened our corporate partners at Redmond City Hall. And then Mayor John Marcioni helped facilitate all the owners to discuss how we can best replicate our version here on the east side. Fast forward a decade later, John and I are both on this board successfully advocating for our to secure hundreds of millions of state and federal funding for the trail corridor and working alongside our corporate and industry partners to help secure their direct investments into the trail. A few weeks ago, we had the opportunity to join forces with bringing our East Trail Partners team with members of the R A C for the first time ever. to discuss the ten-year vision of the East Trail. For us, the Grand Connection processing and the East Trail are integral to each other, creating a seamless regional active transportation network that will serve communities throughout King County for generations to come. For years, our state and region have talked about seamless Vancouver to Vancouver connection. And at the port, we talk about connections from S C A to S C A, connecting our airport with the working Seattle waterfront. But with the growing coordination with our friends in Snohomish County in year six of our organization's operation, we are also aiming to help bridge those connections to the Centennial Trail to the north and the Lake to Sound Trail to the south, while also standing up milestone projects like the Grand Connection. One thing that makes us unique is that we have an opportunity to build it. If you build it, they will come. But building it the right way will make sure that we are building a trail for all of our communities and constituencies, which is a key value of our organization. As you have heard from Tyler, this project represents a strategic equity investment with our countywide impact. And with that, I thank you for your consideration and leadership in advancing regional infrastructure that serves all of our King County residents. Thank you.

[00:13:58 - 00:14:00] Speaker 8
Next is problem.

[00:14:04 - 00:16:37] Speaker 5
Good sunny morning, Chair Benyat Dembowski, Vice Chair Barone, and members of the B F M Committee. My name is Pearl Long, Senior Manager of Public Policy at Amazon, and I am speaking to you today on behalf of Amazon and as co-chair of the Friends of the Grand Connection to express our appreciation for today's briefing on Bellevue's tax increment financing district proposal and to express our strong support for Executive Sahala's ordinance enabling King County's participation in the tax increment area. The Grand Connection Crossing represents a transformative regional infrastructure that will benefit all of King County. The Grand Connection Crossing will create a safe, car-free connection spanning four hundred five linking downtown Bellevue, our region's second largest job center, directly to the forty two-mile East Trail Corridor and one hundred seventy five miles of regional trails. This infrastructure will provide greater car optional access to jobs, housing and recreation for residents across the county. And thanks to King County's leadership, East Trail and Grand Connection Crossing continues to be a top infrastructure priority for Amazon because as the largest employer in King County, we care deeply about advancing sustainable transportation options improving regional connections, and working together to create lasting community assets for generations to come. We have been a committed partner with King County in building this regional trail network, including as the largest private funding partner to help complete the vision for East Trail. We look forward to our continued partnership to maximize East Trail's potential by connecting its 1.6 million million annual users directly to downtown's employment centers, transit hubs, and services. And thanks to recent zoning changes and increasing capacity for significantly more housing in Bellevue, this project will connect fifteen,zero new housing units and including much needed affordable housing, as Brady mentioned, on both sides of the crossing. And this opportunity aligns with Amazon's continued commitment to affordable housing. Since 2021, we have committed over nine hundred million to help create and preserve more than ten,zero homes across the Puget Sound region. Realizing these benefits will rely on strong regional partnerships, such as King County's participation in Bellevue's tax increment area and public-private partnerships. Thank you again for taking time today to hear more about this proposal and how it could unlock regional benefits for King County citizens. Thank you.

[00:16:40 - 00:16:47] Speaker 8
Thank you. Chair Temboski, that is the last person that I have that has raised their hand.

[00:16:40 - 00:16:47] Speaker 8
Thank you. Chair Temboski, that is the last person that I have that has raised their hand.

[00:16:47 - 00:17:50] Speaker 2
All right. Final call. If anybody else wants to offer public comment, raise your hand now. Okay. Nope. All right. We will close public comment. We appreciate you all making time to share those perspectives. And we will turn to our first briefing today of two. We are joined by our Director of the Budget Office, Aaron Rubart. I guess your technical title is Chief Budget Officer, Aaron. Appreciate you being here. I T H I N K M A N Y MEMBERS I F N O T A L L MEMBERS H A V E H A D A N OPPORTUNITY T O M E E T DIRECTLY W I T H EXECUTIVE Z A H A L I A N D M R. R U B A R T T O G E T BRIEFED O N S O M E O F T H E STATUSES O F O U R VARIOUS F U N D S. W E W A N T E D T O B R I N G T H A T FORWARD A S W E L L H E R E T O T H E B U D G E T COMMITTEE ESPECIALLY I N L I G H T O F T H E R E C E N T CHANGES I N T H E S T A T E LEGISLATURE T O O U R FUNDING. and other factors affecting the budget. So this is kind of a look forward on the King County Financial Outlook. And Aaron, we appreciate the request from the executive's office to do this, and we are happy to accommodate it. The floor is yours.

[00:17:51 - 00:18:14] Speaker 3
Thank you, Council Member Dembowski. And good morning to all the members of the B F M committee today. So I am going to share my screen. I have a presentation and talk about King County Financial Outlook. And start that process out. All right, so can I confirm that my screen is being shared?

[00:18:14 - 00:18:16] Speaker 2
It looks great, one hundred.

[00:18:16 - 00:27:48] Speaker 3
Great, thank you. So today I am going to talk about a few things, kind of what has changed since the twenty six-twenty seven budget has passed, what are we seeing with some of our financial plans and funds, health, and then sort of what does the next eighteen to twenty months look like from a budget perspective? I am going to cover this at a pretty high level, open to questions as we go, and also have time for questions at the end, and appreciate the time. So again, I am Aaron Rhubarton, Chief Budget Officer in the Executive Office, and this is the King County Financial Outlook. All right, so what has changed since the twenty six-twenty seven budget passed? In a nutshell, quite a bit. Federal funding, we are going to talk about that. There is changes from the state and then there is changes locally. So on the federal funding side, we continue to monitor what is going to happen with federal funding. We are impacted. There is a preliminary injunction in place, but there are ongoing risks of interrupted funding. This funding is in continuum of care for housing. This is funding from the U S D A for some of our farmland work. This is funding for public health contracts. A lot of that is at risk in different ways, and we are trying to resolve those situations, and we are also trying to plan for those situations. So federal funding continues to be a high level of risk and uncertainty for us. So we are going to continue to monitor that. There is also just uncertainty at the federal level. So tariffs, immigration policies, war in Iran, a lot of this does impact our finances. We think about things like fuel costs, which have gone up and down, currently up at the moment. Interest rates are going, one day they are going to go up, one day they are going to go down. So there is a lot of uncertainty at the federal level that we are trying to plan for. Just to give you some idea, we pay for about forty million, we budget for about forty million in fuel in Metro. We budget for another twelve million from our fleet. We also have lots of fuel uses at our solid waste. as well as through our wastewater function. So fuel is a significant issue. And if costs continue to stay high, we will likely have some sort of supplemental budget request for additional fuel costs just because those costs have gone up. So that is one of the examples of things that we are trying to follow and prepare for. Interest rates, another one. We at the county have the benefit of a A A A bond rating. So our interest rates are pretty low. but they are fluctuating. So as we plan for large capital projects, we need to monitor those rates and make sure that we can lock in low rates as possible. At the state level, there is been a few changes recently, and I am going to go through these in a little more detail, but the millionaire's tax did have an impact on countywide sales tax in the out years of our budget. I will cover that in more detail in a second. And H B two hundred forty two, as covered at the B F M meeting a couple weeks ago, does present some opportunities for the county to change revenue, how it is spent, increase revenue. There is a sales tax option, a public health clinic tax option as well. So those are changes that we are going to be working through over the next few months. There is also been a March O E F A forecast. This was adopted by the forecast council a few weeks ago, about a month ago. Generally positive, except for the impact on sales tax in the out years. So 2029, there is a reduction. And I am going to cover that in a little more detail in a second as well. The other thing is we close our books for 2025. So we see what the year and fund balances are and update all of our financial plans looking forward. So as we start looking at supplemental, as we start looking at fund health, we really use that as an input to evaluate where we are from a fund balance perspective in all of our funds. So a few specific fund outlooks, and I will go into a little more detail on a few of these a few slides later. Most funds are in pretty good shape, but it feels like there is more funds that have projected shortfalls over the next five years than past. So those are the general funds, Public Health Fund, Environmental Health Fund, Animal Services Fund has a shortfall this year. R O A D S has been talked about in multiple forums, has issues in the near term and long term. Permitting Fund is in a negative fund balance right now in 2026, and we are working on how to resolve that for the Permitting Fund. Behavioral Health Fund, is not negative, but has some long-term structural issues. And the housing fund, the Human and Community Development Fund, this is actually a fund that had pretty significant revenue reduction in the most recent forecast that was adopted in the short-term lodging tax. Short-term lodging tax in that fund you know, provides money for projects, provides money for people, provides money for programs. So that is an area where we are going to have to review the impacts of that change. There is other funds that have financial challenges. Wastewater and solid waste, while they do not have negative fund balances, they are really looking at pretty large rate increases to maintain current operations and to meet regulatory requirements. So those have challenges. And then Metro Transit as well, while it does not have an immediate need, does have significant fund balance issues to maintain the current level of service in the out years. So a lot of these funds are in a little worse shape than they have been in the past, from my perspective. But we will have to solve these through the omnibus process and other ways. One of the ways we are addressing the financial challenges and consistent with the executive order on better governance is really tightening some of our financial management. So we are working with agencies who are distressed funds to implement cost saving measures now where possible. We are closely tracking one time expenditures. So one time expenditures, we have had a lot of welcome and needed one time investments of funding that have turned into one time investments and programs, but we need to track those moving forward because those are often not sustainable. So we are really trying to track the one-time expenditures. And one of the areas that my office is working on already for the twenty eight-twenty nine budget is reviewing all the base budgets and starting to develop a conversation, start a conversation about reprioritizing what is in the base budgets. So that is another way that we are going to find savings and present options to leadership across the county to reprioritize the base budget to make sure we are spending dollars where the leaders want to spend the dollars. One note, there might be reductions in the second omnibus. We are still working through that now. But because of some of these challenges, we feel that it might make more sense to start talking about those and having reductions now than waiting for the twenty eight-twenty nine budget. So that is something that I want to signal to this group. There may be reductions necessary in the second. Alluded to before the fiscal impacts of the millionaire's tax. So one thing that happened a few sessions ago was there was an expansion of the sales tax base. So in our twenty six-twenty seven budget, that resulted in about one hundred fifty seven million more in sales tax revenue. That is in our budget now. That is what we are planning for in twenty six-27. That has not necessarily changed. What has changed is the millionaire's tax is going to take that expansion out, or most of it out, and that leads to a reduction in sales tax beginning in 2029. Now, this has been incorporated into our most recent sales tax forecasts. It is going to be incorporated into the financial projections we are working on. But this is a new change. And this begins in 2029. I heard that maybe it is not going to happen as is, or we are going to look at some changes. But as of now, we are planning for this reduction in 2029. From a dollar perspective, what that means is roughly fifty seven million less for transit in 2029, eighteen million less for the general fund in 2029. and five million less for our health or housing, our Doors Open program, and our mental health program in 2029 and beyond. So pretty significant reduction in 2029. That is what we are starting to work through with all of our forecasts.

[00:27:49 - 00:27:55] Speaker 2
And Aaron, I will jump in here. I did not ask you, would you like questions as we go along, or would you prefer that we

[00:27:55 - 00:27:58] Speaker 3
wait? Questions as we go. Okay.

[00:27:59 - 00:28:02] Speaker 2
I do not have any, but let us see if any members do. Council Member Perry.

[00:28:04 - 00:29:51] Speaker 10
Thank you, Chair Demboski. Thank you, Director Ruppert. Would you mind going back one slide? One more. As the legislative branch and the check and balance of the executive branch, it is really helpful to have the details of what we are talking about. So the things that are in your big money brain around projected shortfalls over the next five years, having actual figures next to these to the right in a chart that says general fund, you were saying that these are larger than you, a bit larger, and I do not know what a bit means to someone who is working with a twenty billion budget, so If you could help me understand as a follow-up what this looked like for the last review, what it looks like going forward. When you say the next five years, some of these are fine, but there is a year in which you think there might be huge impacts to behavioral health, housing right now, the roads, really giving us a sense of what we are talking about this year so that we have that and the public has that so that when we are looking at the omnibus, we can make some educated decisions that have some hard numbers with it. And I also think when we are talking about dollars, it is just so important to have the actual things and then to be able to compare them to that level of concern to be able to make appropriate decisions because I think we are going to have some tough choices coming up. So that would be very helpful if you could get that to us offline. I would really appreciate it, or at least to me if others are

[00:29:52 - 00:50:59] Speaker 3
comfortable with that. Absolutely. We will be happy to share that. And I think what I will pull together or have the team pull together is And sometimes that is very good. We see big increases in sales tax, and typically we identify programs to spend that on. We also see decreases in sales tax, not just sales tax is not keeping up with inflation, but actual pretty large decreases. So those are not built in in the event that we do have a recession or even a mild downturn in the regional economy. Sales tax would go down, and that impacts the general fund. That impacts transit. That impacts our human services. Our lodging tax is another one. So those are not built in. We will have more clarity on that in July. And then again, March, we get pretty good updates and information from our forecast, our Office of Economic and Financial Analysis, who send out updates that we look at pretty closely. But that is a risk. that we have to collectively monitor, both the cost side and the revenue side.

[00:51:01 - 00:51:20] Speaker 2
Thank you. Okay. Thank you, Aaron. Anything else? Well, thanks for that uplifting and optimistic presentation. Anything you would like to say to wrap up on this one, Aaron?

[00:51:21 - 00:52:04] Speaker 3
Well, I think we are in, you know, challenging times across the board. I do think that we need to really focus on leveraging our strengths. And we do have a twenty billion budget. So we have resources. We have great leadership, the council and the executive and our department directors. We have great partnerships with labor. So we have the benefit of pretty, while it does fluctuate year to year, pretty stable long-term finances. and a really good credit rating so i think all those things we need to leverage to kind of collectively solve these issues um and so i want to leave that on a positive note that i think we can do that

[00:52:05 - 00:52:08] Speaker 2
all right very good on councilmember mosqueda

[00:52:11 - 00:53:05] Speaker 4
Thanks. To that point, I think I appreciate the briefings that you did with each of the offices as you walked us through the scenarios that you just presented. It would be helpful to have kind of a side-by-side comparison of all the tools allowed for our disposal, whether that is increasing the levy amounts, as you noted, the public health tax, the sales tax option that we were considering for roads. whether it means going out and asking for our existing levies to be renewed sooner. It would be really helpful to see sort of a list of revenue options, even though we are very limited in our revenue tools. And again, continue to ask the state legislature to give us more local, more less regressive revenue tools. But it would be helpful to have that menu of options to see how those potential tools, if deployed, could help plug the budget holes and to have that sequenced by year.

[00:53:06 - 00:54:21] Speaker 2
Yep. Thanks. Definitely do that. Happy to. Okay. Anything further? No? All right, Aaron. Appreciate that. I think you are also on deck for our second item of Today's agenda, and that is the ordinance that has been transmitted by the executive to participate in a text increment financing proposal in the city of Bellevue. We heard public testimony about the grand connections project today. The request for us is less about the project and more about the financing. And so this is on a fairly, well, there is a specific schedule that needs to be met. And normally we would wait to have a briefing until we had our central staff do an analysis and prepare a report and be able to present. But because of the uniqueness of this issue, the complexity and the timeframe, we wanted to bring it forward for an initial briefing on today's agenda. We are A C T I N G A L I T T L E B I T OUTSIDE O F O U R N O R M A L PROTOCOLS H E R E T O FACILITATE R E V I E W O F T H I S A N D A S K I N G T H E EXECUTIVE, Y O U, A A R O N, T O G I V E T H E INITIAL OVERVIEW. S O W I T H T H A T, We will T U R N We will H A N D T H E F L O O R B A C K O V E R T O Y O

[00:54:22 - 00:54:39] Speaker 3
U. N E W SPEAKER T H A N K Y O U. S O I am G O I N G T O S H A R E M Y S C R E E N A G A I N F O R A DIFFERENT PRESENTATION T H I S T I M E. And just confirming that that is on the screen.

[00:54:39 - 00:54:40] Speaker 2
Looks good.

[00:54:41 - 00:58:38] Speaker 3
So this is an overview of the proposed tax increment financing proposal for Bellevue. I am going to go into some of the details. This is an overview. This is an area that we do not do a lot of work on TIFs. There is not a lot of TIFs in the region. I think there is three currently. And this is a proposal specific to Bellevue that was transmitted yesterday. So high level, and I am going to cover this at a pretty high level and then get into a couple of the details on this proposal, open it up for questions. And then I do expect that there will be more detailed questions over the next couple of weeks that we will answer. So tax increment financing, otherwise known as T I F, It is a financing tool for local governments to fund infrastructure and redevelopment projects. Promotes development through private development and new construction. What does that really mean, though? How is this working for the county? One, there is a jurisdiction that defines a geographic area, establishes a defined geographic area. In this case, there are about thirty parcels in Bellevue. have been identified it is a roughly sixty acres or so parcels and that is the area that the county assessor will essentially draw a circle around around those parcels then there is an investment so there is an investment by a jurisdiction to make public improvements within that area so within that circle there is improvements that are made and then the property taxes of those parcels in that area increase. And they increase to such a way that over time, they will be able to pay back for that initial investment for those public improvements. At a very high level, that is how this works. A little more detail on how it impacts property taxes. And feel free to ask questions as we go along here. So one thing the T I F does, it relaxes the one property tax within that specific geographic area. So on this illustration, we have the base value. That is the base value of all of those roughly thirty parcels that are in the T I F area. The county will still collect property taxes on the base values in that area. That is not going to change. However, when the T I F is implemented, that is frozen for the county, and then any increase in the value of those parcels is used to fund the public infrastructure improvements. So all of those property taxes, instead of increasing and going to the county, the incremental increase will go to the city under this proposal, and that will be used to fund the public infrastructure proposal that they are making. So this is the Grand Connection. This is for roughly twenty five years. So the incremental increases and the agreement last for twenty five years. So in year one, there is some amount that goes to help pay off, in this case, the bonds for the T I F. In year two, there is a little more. Year three, year four. Our proposal has a cap on how much can actually be sent over in this T I F, but That is roughly how it works. And I would love to answer any questions on this because I know it is a unique financing structure that is being proposed here.

Okay. So this is a little more detail on the fiscal side of the Bellevue T I F or the county. So this T I F, there is a one-time opportunity to implement the T I F downtown by state law. This opportunity expires in June of this year. So hence the urgency and the timeline. This T I F would fund the Grand Connection Crossing, which we heard about earlier. which includes a pedestrian and bicycle crossing over four hundred five, which connects downtown Bellevue with Wilberton and East Rail. It does require an opt-in from the taxing districts. That is what we are talking about here as a county. And then this would go into effect in February 2027, and the first change to the property taxes would be in 2028. So the county's role in the T I F, the county may opt in or opt out. And the proposed ordinance essentially opts the county in to the T I F. So it is about sixty three acres of parcels in downtown Bellevue and Wilberton, which will be frozen at the 2026 levels where they are today. The proposal includes up to thirty million in property taxes. that would go to the T I F between 2027 and 2058, assuming the cap on the county contributions. And then again, after twenty five years, property tax collections revert back to all the districts. So one of the issues on, or not one of the issues, one of the conditions of the county's proposal is it sets a thirty million maximum on the amount of funding going to the T I F. This does impact a few of our property tax funds, and I want to be very clear on this. So the general funds would send roughly twenty one million to the T I F over that twenty five years. There is a few other small property tax. These are all non-voter-approved property taxes, veteran services, behavioral health, developmental disabilities, a couple hundred thousand dollars for each of those properties, property tax levies. Metro Transit would be about two million over the timeframe, and the hospital levy would be about six million over the timeframe. So these are the property taxes, non-voter approved property taxes that are included in the T I F proposal. And this is roughly thirty million over twenty five years. Some of the safeguards I mentioned, maximum of thirty million in property tax, would be redirected to the T I F. Voter-approved levies are exempt. They are not included on this. Only councilmanic levies are included. However, in the proposed ordinance, there is language that beginning in 2041, voter-approved levies could be added to this if that was needed to get to the thirty million that is being committed here. So that would have to be written into those property tax, those voter-approved property levies as an applicable use, but that would be an option in 2041. City of Bellevue does reimburse the county for assessor and treasury costs, and there are a series of annual reporting requirements written into the ordinance as well. Any questions on those?
